title: Typography
summary: Images and copy work together to convey a message. But we don’t get all the way without the right typography.

columns.text: We see thousands of messages every day – what distinguishes the good from the bad is often typography. A brand that pays attention to every detail is perceived as more professional and trustworthy. The copy should feel effortless to read.
Our typeface SEB SansSerif is specially designed specifically for us. It's created for maximum legibility in digital channels while being equally efficient in print.
SEB SansSerif blends aesthetics and functionality from both the classic and modern typography to create a typeface with a timeless, distinct, and unique expression.
Think about the words. And use as few as possible. Work with the design, typography, and image to make the message as clear as possible.
SEB SansSerif blends aesthetics and functionality from both the classic and modern typographical eras to create a typeface with a timeless, distinct and unique expression.
Typographical weights are a powerful tool to build hierarchies. Used correctly they will help strengthen our words and communication as well as establish a distinct visual recognition of our brand.
Keep in mind that minimising the amount of weights and sizes in texts makes for a more unified and neat look. It’s also calming for the eyes and helps the reader to take in the information.
SEB SansSerif Regular—Use this for body copy.
SEB SansSerif Book—Use Book both for headlines and shorter texts.
SEB SansSerif Medium—Medium can be used to highlight words.
SEB SansSerif Bold—Use to establish design hierarchies and in sub headers.
Do use Book whenever a text is large – both for headlines and larger texts. It carries SEBs brand identity.
Don't use Bold or Medium as headline fonts.
Do use Medium and Italic to highlight words and sections of body copy. As a rule of thumb, use Italic in texts with a narrative and Medium in more technical and informational texts.
Don't write in CAPITALS. We never do that.
Don't use italics in larger texts. Even though we have Bold and Medium as Italics,  they should only be used in very specific cases.
Do use Bold for sub headlines within body text, or to create contrast in informational systems like tables and data presentation. Never use it in big headlines.
Don't use Light. It is still active in some designs, but should never be used in new material.

Left aligned (or ragged right) is the most readable alignment – it provides the eye with a common starting point for each line. Make sure the lines aren’t too long. Aim for 7-10 words or 45-65 characters per line.
The key to a pleasant and well designed text is kerning. Our typeface need some love to look good. See examples to see the difference.
The key to a pleasant paragraph is to give each line it’s own space. This can be achieved by using at least 120% line-height. In smaller text going up to 140% may be necessary.
Calculation of good line-height—Font-size x 1.4 = line-heightE.g. 12 x 1,4 = 16,8 line-height

In some cases, centered text works better. This is usually in digital contexts, advertising or in situations where it helps to present information in a more logical way than left-aligned text. In advertising, a centered layout creates a more stable and confident look.

We use Arial as a system font. A system font is a typeface that is available on all computers. When sending open documents, such as Word or PowerPoint documents, always use Arial.We use Arial Bold as a replacement for SEB SansSerif Bold and Arial Regular as a replacement for SEB SansSerif Regular.
DoAlways use SEB SansSerif.
Don'tNever use any other typeface.
DoUse left-align text.
Don'tNever us color on text.
DoKern and space the typography with care.
Don'tDon't add extreme kerning or spacing.
DoUse a line height that makes for readable text.
Don'tTo much (or too little) line height will make the text hard to read.
